Pre-competitive basic research is essential to the semiconductor industry and the first step in the semiconductor production process. Semiconductors are all around us. _The semiconductor industry is leading the greatest period of progress in history._ The semiconductor industry has consistently prioritized R&D – even during difficult economic times and throughout fluctuations in sales revenue – because chip designers and manufacturers know that these investments will pay off down the road. What is a Semiconductor?
========================

Semiconductors are the Brains of Modern Electronics
---------------------------------------------------

Semiconductors are an essential component of electronic devices, enabling advances in communications, computing, healthcare, military systems, transportation, clean energy, and countless other applications.

[View Industry Fact Sheet >](https://www.semiconductors.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/SIA-Industry-Factsheet-2024.pdf)

Stages of Semiconductor Production
----------------------------------

Step 1: Research
----------------

Pre-competitive basic research is essential to the semiconductor industry and the first step in the semiconductor production process.

Step 2: Design
--------------

Engineers use highly sophisticated equipment to design semiconductors,similar to how architects design buildings.

Step 3: Raw Materials
---------------------

Many semiconductors start out as sand, which contains a large amount of silicon, but other pure materials can also be used.

Step 4: Ingot
-------------

The sand is purified and melted into solid cylinders called ingots, weighing up to 200+ lbs.

Step 5: Blank Wafer
-------------------

The ingot is then sliced into very thin (1 mm) silicon discs and polished to a flawless finish.

Step 6: Finished Wafer
----------------------

Next, wafers are printed with highly intricate circuit designs that will later become individual chips.

Step 7: Cut Wafer
-----------------

The silicon wafer containing finished semiconductors, sometimes as many as 70,000 per wafer, is then cut up into tiny individual semiconductors called dies.

Step 8: Packaged Chip
---------------------

These dies are then packaged into finished semiconductors, which can be placed into devices.

Step 9: Chip on Circuit Board
-----------------------------

Finished semiconductors are embedded in countless electronics devices, from computers and smartphones to highly advanced medical equipment and supercomputers.

Semiconductors, sometimes referred to as integrated circuits (ICs) or microchips, are made from pure elements, typically silicon or germanium, or compounds such as gallium arsenide. In a process called doping, small amounts of impurities are added to these pure elements, causing large changes in the conductivity of the material.

Due to their role in the fabrication of electronic devices, semiconductors are an important part of our lives. Imagine life without electronic devices. There would be no smartphones, radios, TVs, computers, video games, or advanced medical diagnostic equipment.

Developments in semiconductor technology during the past 50 years have made electronic devices smaller, faster, and more reliable. Think for a minute of all the encounters you have with electronic devices. How many have you seen or used in the last 24 hours? Each has important components that have been manufactured with electronic materials.

A single semiconductor chip has as many transistors as all of the stones in the Great Pyramid in Giza, and today there are more than 100 billion integrated circuits in daily use around the world—that’s equal to the number of stars in our corner of the Milky Way galaxy.

It is truly a modern marvel, a feat of human ingenuity and engineering unmatched by any other industry.

Semiconductor firms generally organize their activities around the two main stages of semiconductor production: design and manufacturing. Companies that focus only on design are referred to as “fabless” firms, while companies that focus only on manufacturing are called “foundries.” Semiconductor firms that do both are called Integrated Device Manufacturers, or IDMs.

Semiconductors Improve Our Lives

_Without semiconductors, the technology that we count on every day would not be possible._

Semiconductors are all around us. They control the computers we use to conduct business, the phones and mobile devices we use to communicate, the cars and planes that get us from place to place, the machines that diagnose and treat illnesses, the military systems that protect us, and the electronic gadgets we use to listen to music, watch movies, and play games, just to name a few.

And not only does semiconductor technology make these devices possible, it also makes them more compact, less expensive, and more powerful. For example, in 1984, mobile phones weighed about 2 lbs., cost around $4,000, and held a charge for only about 30 minutes of talk time.

At SIA, a common theme of the initiatives we support – policies to drive innovation, research programs of our affiliated organizations, etc. – is that all of them are intended to maintain and accelerate advancements in our industry. Working together, we can ensure that semiconductors continue to improve our lives.

Semiconductors Build Our Future

_The semiconductor industry is leading the greatest period of progress in history._

We have followed Moore’s Law to once unimaginable levels, and semiconductors have revolutionized the way we work, communicate, travel, entertain, harness energy, and treat illness, just to name a few.

And our greatest potential still lies ahead. As the building blocks of technology, semiconductors will continue to enable the world’s greatest breakthroughs. From aerospace and consumer electronics to energy and medicine, entire industries will be transformed.

The semiconductor industry has a longstanding tradition of partnering with government to spur innovation and build a bridge to the future. In the 1980s, the industry teamed with the government to establish SEMATECH, which sponsors advanced semiconductor manufacturing research and is now recognized by many as the ideal model of public-private collaboration.

The industry also helps fund cutting-edge university research through the Semiconductor Research Corporation (SRC), the world’s leading university research consortium for semiconductors and related technologies. SRC has launched hugely successful **[government-industry-university research partnerships](https://www.semiconductors.org/policies/research/)** that are unmatched in size and scope by programs in other industries.

U.S. semiconductor companies annually invest about one-fifth of total revenue in R&D – among the highest rates of any industry. The semiconductor industry has consistently prioritized R&D – even during difficult economic times and throughout fluctuations in sales revenue – because chip designers and manufacturers know that these investments will pay off down the road. The federal government should maintain its share of the partnership and fund scientific research at sustainable levels.

Through collaboration, and effective government policies, America will continue to lead the world and the semiconductor industry will continue to create jobs, drive economic growth, and develop the technologies needed to build our future.

Semiconductors Strengthen Our Country

_A strong semiconductor industry is vital to America’s economic strength, national security, and global competitiveness._

Semiconductors are a foundational technology for virtually all areas of our economy. Semiconductors were invented in America, and the U.S. still leads the world in cutting-edge manufacturing and design.

The semiconductor industry directly employs over 250,000 workers in the United States and and supports nearly 1.8 million additional U.S. jobs.

Semiconductors are a top-5 American export, after airplanes, refined oil, and automobiles.

The key to maintaining the advancements that fuel our industry and the U.S. economy is research. Unfortunately, U.S. investments in R&D as a share of GDP have decreased in recent decades.

SIA member companies continue to invest and expand in the U.S., with the construction of new and expanded state-of-the-art fabrication facilities across the country. Overall, U.S.-based semiconductor companies retain about 50 percent of global market share in a highly competitive market.

A thriving U.S. semiconductor industry means a strong American economy, high-paying jobs and an outsized impact across the nation. Simply put, semiconductors strengthen our country.

### How do semiconductors impact your world?

[Find out >](https://www.semiconductors.org/semiconductors-101/industry-impact/)

# The Many Applications of Semiconductors

![](https://reboundeu.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/rebound-blog-32-featured-image-640x360-c-default.jpg)

Most people have heard of microchips, but how much do you know about the components that make them possible? Here we will be discussing the many applications of semiconductors.

## **What is a semiconductor material?**

A semiconductor is a material that has conductivity properties between a conductor, which has the ability to conduct electricity, and a non-conductor or insulator, which doesn’t allow electrical charges to flow through it.

This means that, under certain conditions, semiconductors have the ability to act either as a conductor or an insulator. Semiconductors can be pure elements such as the most commonly used silicon, or compounds such as gallium arsenide.

## **Semiconductor devices**

The main application of semiconductors is in the creation of semiconductor devices which are essential for a multitude of electronic products. Semiconductor devices conduct electric currents in the solid state, instead of as free electrons across a vacuum, and are the successor to vacuum tubes in almost all applications.

One of the most commonly used semiconductor components is the diode, which acts as a one-way valve in a circuit, only allowing the flow of current in one direction. Conversely, conductors allow current to flow in both directions.

Semiconductors are also used in the design of transistors, which are used both for fast switching and for current amplification.

The MOSFET (metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistor) is the most common semiconductor device in the world.

## **Semiconductors and microchips**

The semiconductor silicon serves as the base material for the microchip, also known as an integrated circuit or monolithic integrated circuit, a component used in almost every modern electronic device.

A huge amount of tiny MOSFETs are used in the creation of microchips. They are made up of a set of electronic circuits on a small, flat piece of silicon, with many transistors that can turn a current on or off.

The two main types of microchips are the logic chip and the memory chip. Logic chips process information to help electronic devices complete their tasks. One of the most widely known and commonly used logic chips is the CPU or central processing unit.

Memory chips, on the other hand, store information and come in two types – NAND Flash, and ‘working memory’ chips known as DRAM.

The demand for microchips is so high that there is currently a huge supply issue causing lengthy lead times and availability issues across the electronics industry.

## **Semiconductor applications**

Semiconductors are used in almost every sector of electronics.

**Consumer electronics:** Mobile phones, laptops, games consoles, microwaves and refrigerators all operate with the use of semiconductor components such as integrated chips, diodes and transistors. The high demand for these devices is part of the reason there are currently such long wait times for many consumer electronic devices.

**Embedded systems:** Embedded systems are small computers that form part of a larger machine. They can control the device and allow user interaction. Embedded systems that we commonly use include central heating systems, digital watches, GPS systems, fitness trackers, televisions and engine management systems in vehicles.

**Thermal conductivity:** Some semiconductors have high thermal conductivity, so can be used as a cooling agent in certain thermoelectric applications.

**Lighting and LED displays:** Some semiconductors, usually those available in liquid or amorphous form as a thin-coated film, can produce light and are used in LEDs and OLEDs.

**Solar cells:** Silicon is also the most commonly used semiconductor in the production of solar panel cells.

 That concludes our brief guide to the applications of semiconductors. As you can see, semiconductors are integral to the modern world and play an important role in the electronic devices we use or come into contact with every day.

## **What is a semiconductor?**

A semiconductor is a material whose ability to conduct electricity falls between that of a conductor (which readily conducts electricity) and an insulator (which resists electricity). Commonly made from silicon, semiconductors can function as either depending on their environment.

## **FAQs**

**What are the main uses of semiconductor devices?**

Semiconductor devices are crucial in various electronic products. They are used in diodes to allow current flow in one direction, in transistors for switching and amplification, and in microchips which form the foundation of many modern electronic devices.

**How are semiconductors used in consumer electronics?**

Semiconductors form an essential part of many consumer electronics like mobile phones, laptops, and microwaves. They are used in integrated circuits and components like diodes and transistors that help these devices function efficiently.

**What is the future outlook for semiconductors in smartphones?**

The semiconductor industry is constantly evolving with significant innovations [specifically designed for smartphones](https://reboundeu.com/insights/blog/the-future-of-semi-conductors-for-smartphones/). This includes advancements in microchip technology to enhance processing power and energy efficiency, which are critical for supporting sophisticated mobile applications.

**How are semiconductors impacting renewable energy technologies?**

Semiconductors are playing a pivotal role in the development of renewable energy technologies, particularly in solar power. Silicon, a primary material in semiconductors, is extensively used in solar cells to improve efficiency and reduce costs, aiding the growth of sustainable energy solutions.

--- SOURCE 5: Semiconductor ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semiconductor

SUMMARY:
Semiconductors with high thermal conductivity can be used for heat dissipation and improving thermal management of electronics. They play a crucial role in

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Semiconductor

A **semiconductor** is a material with [electrical conductivity](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity") between that of a [conductor](/wiki/Electrical_conductor "Electrical conductor") and an [insulator](/wiki/Insulator_(electricity) "Insulator (electricity)").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Its conductivity can be modified by adding impurities ("[doping](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)")") to its [crystal structure](/wiki/Crystal_structure "Crystal structure"). When two regions with different doping levels are present in the same crystal, they form a [semiconductor junction](/wiki/Semiconductor_junction "Semiconductor junction").

The behavior of [charge carriers](/wiki/Charge_carrier "Charge carrier"), which include [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron"), [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion"), and [electron holes](/wiki/Electron_hole "Electron hole"), at these junctions is the basis of [diodes](/wiki/Diode "Diode"), [transistors](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor"), and most modern [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"). Some examples of semiconductors are [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon"), [germanium](/wiki/Germanium "Germanium"), [gallium arsenide](/wiki/Gallium_arsenide "Gallium arsenide"), and elements near the so-called "[metalloid staircase](/wiki/Metalloid_staircase "Metalloid staircase")" on the [periodic table](/wiki/Periodic_table "Periodic table"). After silicon, gallium arsenide is the second-most common semiconductor and is used in [laser diodes](/wiki/Laser_diode "Laser diode"), [solar cells](/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell"), microwave-frequency [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit"), and others. Silicon is a critical element for fabricating most [electronic circuits](/wiki/Electronic_circuit "Electronic circuit").

[Semiconductor devices](/wiki/Semiconductor_device "Semiconductor device") can display a range of different useful properties, such as passing current more easily in one direction than the other, showing variable resistance, and having sensitivity to light or heat. Because the electrical properties of a semiconductor material can be modified by doping and by the application of electrical fields or light, devices made from semiconductors can be used for amplification, switching, and [energy conversion](/wiki/Energy_conversion "Energy conversion"). The term semiconductor is also used to describe materials used in high capacity, medium- to [high-voltage cables](/wiki/High-voltage_cable "High-voltage cable") as part of their insulation, and these materials are often plastic XLPE ([cross-linked polyethylene](/wiki/Cross-linked_polyethylene "Cross-linked polyethylene")) with carbon black.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The conductivity of silicon can be increased by adding a small amount (of the order of 1 in 108) of pentavalent ([antimony](/wiki/Antimony "Antimony"), [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus"), or [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic "Arsenic")) or trivalent ([boron](/wiki/Boron "Boron"), [gallium](/wiki/Gallium "Gallium"), [indium](/wiki/Indium "Indium")) atoms.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This process is known as doping, and the resulting semiconductors are known as doped or [extrinsic semiconductors](/wiki/Extrinsic_semiconductor "Extrinsic semiconductor"). Apart from doping, the conductivity of a semiconductor can be improved by increasing its temperature. This is contrary to the behavior of a metal, in which conductivity decreases with an increase in temperature.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The modern understanding of the properties of a semiconductor relies on [quantum physics](/wiki/Quantum_physics "Quantum physics") to explain the movement of charge carriers in a [crystal lattice](/wiki/Crystal_structure "Crystal structure").[[5]](#cite_note-Feynman-5) Doping greatly increases the number of charge carriers within the crystal. When a semiconductor is doped by Group V elements, they will behave like [donors](/wiki/Donor_(semiconductors) "Donor (semiconductors)") creating free [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron"), known as "[n-type](/wiki/Extrinsic_semiconductor#N-type_semiconductors "Extrinsic semiconductor")" doping. When a semiconductor is doped by Group III elements, they will behave like [acceptors](/wiki/Acceptor_(semiconductors) "Acceptor (semiconductors)") creating free holes, known as "[p-type](/wiki/Extrinsic_semiconductor#P-type_semiconductors "Extrinsic semiconductor")" doping. The semiconductor materials used in electronic devices are doped under precise conditions to control the concentration and regions of p- and n-type dopants. A single semiconductor device [crystal](/wiki/Crystal "Crystal") can have many p- and n-type regions; the [p–n junctions](/wiki/P%E2%80%93n_junction "P–n junction") between these regions are responsible for the useful electronic behavior. Using a [hot-point probe](/wiki/Hot-point_probe "Hot-point probe"), one can determine quickly whether a semiconductor sample is p- or n-type.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

A few of the properties of semiconductor materials were observed throughout the mid-19th and first decades of the 20th century. The first practical application of semiconductors in electronics was the 1904 development of the [cat's-whisker detector](/wiki/Cat%27s-whisker_detector "Cat's-whisker detector"), a primitive semiconductor diode used in early [radio](/wiki/Wireless_telegraphy "Wireless telegraphy") receivers. Developments in quantum physics led in turn to the invention of the [transistor](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor") in 1947[[7]](#cite_note-7) and the [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") in 1958.

## Contents

## Properties

### Variable electrical conductivity

Semiconductors in their natural state are poor conductors because a [current](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current") requires flow of electrons, and semiconductors have their [valence bands](/wiki/Valence_band "Valence band") filled, preventing the entire flow of new electrons. Several developed techniques allow semiconducting materials to behave like conducting materials, such as [doping](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)") or [gating](/wiki/Field_effect_(semiconductor) "Field effect (semiconductor)"). These modifications have two outcomes: **n-type** and **p-type**. These refer to the excess or shortage of electrons, respectively. A balanced number of electrons would cause a current to flow throughout the material.[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

### Homojunctions

**[Homojunctions](/wiki/Homojunction "Homojunction")** occur when two differently doped semiconducting materials are joined. For example, a configuration could consist of p-doped and n-doped [germanium](/wiki/Germanium "Germanium"). This results in an exchange of electrons and holes between the differently doped semiconducting materials. The n-doped germanium would have an excess of electrons, and the p-doped germanium would have an excess of holes. The transfer occurs until an equilibrium is reached by a process called [recombination](/wiki/Recombination_(physics) "Recombination (physics)"), which causes the migrating electrons from the n-type to come in contact with the migrating holes from the p-type.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The result of this process is a narrow strip of immobile [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion"), which causes an [electric field](/wiki/Electric_field "Electric field") across the junction.[[5]](#cite_note-Feynman-5)[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

### Excited electrons

A difference in the electric potential on a semiconducting material would cause it to leave thermal equilibrium and create a non-equilibrium situation. This introduces electrons and holes to the system, which interact via a process called [ambipolar diffusion](/wiki/Ambipolar_diffusion "Ambipolar diffusion"). Whenever thermal equilibrium is disturbed in a semiconducting material, the number of holes and electrons changes. Such disruptions can occur as a result of a temperature difference or [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon"), which can enter the system and create electrons and holes. The processes that create or annihilate electrons and holes are called [generation](/wiki/Carrier_generation_and_recombination "Carrier generation and recombination") and recombination, respectively.[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

### Light emission

In certain semiconductors, excited electrons can relax by emitting light instead of producing heat.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Controlling the semiconductor composition and [electrical current](/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current") allows for the manipulation of the emitted light's properties.[[11]](#cite_note-11) These semiconductors are used in the construction of [light-emitting diodes](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") and fluorescent [quantum dots](/wiki/Quantum_dot "Quantum dot").

### High thermal conductivity

Semiconductors with high thermal conductivity can be used for heat dissipation and improving [thermal management](/wiki/Thermal_management_(electronics) "Thermal management (electronics)") of electronics. They play a crucial role in [electric vehicles](/wiki/Electric_vehicle "Electric vehicle"), high-brightness [LEDs](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") and [power modules](/wiki/Power_module "Power module"), among other applications.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Thermal energy conversion

Semiconductors have large [thermoelectric power factors](/wiki/Thermoelectric_power_factor "Thermoelectric power factor") making them useful in [thermoelectric generators](/wiki/Thermoelectric_generator "Thermoelectric generator"), as well as high [thermoelectric figures of merit](/wiki/Thermoelectric_figure_of_merit "Thermoelectric figure of merit") making them useful in [thermoelectric coolers](/wiki/Thermoelectric_cooler "Thermoelectric cooler").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Materials

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Silicon.jpg/250px-Silicon.jpg)

A large number of elements and compounds have semiconducting properties, including:[[16]](#cite_note-Yacobi03-16)

The most common semiconducting materials are crystalline solids, but [amorphous](/wiki/Amorphous_silicon "Amorphous silicon") and liquid semiconductors are also known. These include [hydrogenated amorphous silicon](/wiki/Hydrogenated_amorphous_silicon "Hydrogenated amorphous silicon") and mixtures of [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic "Arsenic"), [selenium](/wiki/Selenium "Selenium"), and [tellurium](/wiki/Tellurium "Tellurium") in a variety of proportions. These compounds share with better-known semiconductors the properties of intermediate conductivity and a rapid variation of conductivity with temperature, as well as occasional [negative resistance](/wiki/Negative_resistance "Negative resistance"). Such disordered materials lack the rigid crystalline structure of conventional semiconductors such as silicon. They are generally used in [thin film](/wiki/Thin_film "Thin film") structures, which do not require material of higher electronic quality, being relatively insensitive to impurities and radiation damage.

### Preparation of semiconductor materials

Almost all of today's electronic technology involves the use of semiconductors, with the most important aspect being the [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (IC), which are found in [desktops](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer"), [laptops](/wiki/Laptop_computer "Laptop computer"), scanners, [cell-phones](/wiki/Cell-phone "Cell-phone"), and other electronic devices. Semiconductors for ICs are mass-produced. To create an ideal semiconducting material, chemical purity is paramount. Any small imperfection can have a drastic effect on how the semiconducting material behaves due to the scale at which the materials are used.[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

A high degree of crystalline perfection is also required, since faults in the crystal structure (such as [dislocations](/wiki/Dislocation "Dislocation"), [twins](/wiki/Crystal_twinning "Crystal twinning"), and [stacking faults](/wiki/Stacking_fault "Stacking fault")) interfere with the semiconducting properties of the material. Crystalline faults are a major cause of defective semiconductor devices. The larger the crystal, the more difficult it is to achieve the necessary perfection. Current mass production processes use crystal [ingots](/wiki/Ingot "Ingot") between 100 and 300 mm (3.9 and 11.8 in) in diameter, grown as cylinders and sliced into [wafers](/wiki/Wafer_(electronics) "Wafer (electronics)"). The round shape characteristic of these wafers comes from [single-crystal ingots](/wiki/Boule_(crystal) "Boule (crystal)") usually produced using the [Czochralski method](/wiki/Czochralski_method "Czochralski method"). Silicon wafers were first introduced in the 1940s.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

There is a combination of processes that are used to prepare semiconducting materials for ICs. One process is called [thermal oxidation](/wiki/Thermal_oxidation "Thermal oxidation"), which forms [silicon dioxide](/wiki/Silicon_dioxide "Silicon dioxide") on the surface of the [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon"). This is used as a [gate insulator](/wiki/Gate_dielectric "Gate dielectric") and [field oxide](/wiki/LOCOS "LOCOS"). Other processes are called [photomasks](/wiki/Photomask "Photomask") and [photolithography](/wiki/Photolithography "Photolithography"). This process is what creates the patterns on the circuit in the integrated circuit. [Ultraviolet light](/wiki/Ultraviolet_light "Ultraviolet light") is used along with a [photoresist](/wiki/Photoresist "Photoresist") layer to create a chemical change that generates the patterns for the circuit.[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

The etching is the next process that is required. The part of the silicon that was not covered by the [photoresist](/wiki/Photoresist "Photoresist") layer from the previous step can now be etched. The main process typically used today is called [plasma etching](/wiki/Plasma_etching "Plasma etching"). Plasma etching usually involves an [etch gas](/wiki/Plasma_etching "Plasma etching") pumped in a low-pressure chamber to create [plasma](/wiki/Plasma_(physics) "Plasma (physics)"). A common etch gas is [chlorofluorocarbon](/wiki/Chlorofluorocarbon "Chlorofluorocarbon"), or more commonly known [Freon](/wiki/Freon "Freon"). A high [radio-frequency](/wiki/Radio-frequency "Radio-frequency") [voltage](/wiki/Voltage "Voltage") between the [cathode](/wiki/Cathode "Cathode") and [anode](/wiki/Anode "Anode") is what creates the plasma in the chamber. The [silicon wafer](/wiki/Wafer_(electronics) "Wafer (electronics)") is located on the cathode, which causes it to be hit by the positively charged ions that are released from the plasma. The result is silicon that is etched [anisotropically](/wiki/Anisotropy "Anisotropy").[[5]](#cite_note-Feynman-5)[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

The last process is called [diffusion](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)"). This is the process that gives the semiconducting material its desired semiconducting properties. It is also known as [doping](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)"). The process introduces an impure atom to the system, which creates the [p–n junction](/wiki/P%E2%80%93n_junction "P–n junction"). To get the impure atoms embedded in the silicon wafer, the wafer is first put in a 1,100 degree Celsius chamber. The atoms are injected in and eventually diffuse with the silicon. After the process is completed and the silicon has reached room temperature, the doping process is done and the semiconducting [wafer](/wiki/Wafer_(electronics) "Wafer (electronics)") is almost prepared.[[5]](#cite_note-Feynman-5)[[8]](#cite_note-Neamen-8)

## Physics of semiconductors

### Energy bands and electrical conduction

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Band_filling_diagram.svg/400px-Band_filling_diagram.svg.png)

Semiconductors are defined by their unique electric conductive behavior, somewhere between that of a conductor and an insulator.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The differences between these materials can be understood in terms of the [quantum states](/wiki/Quantum_state "Quantum state") for electrons, each of which may contain zero or one electron (by the [Pauli exclusion principle](/wiki/Pauli_exclusion_principle "Pauli exclusion principle")). These states are associated with the [electronic band structure](/wiki/Electronic_band_structure "Electronic band structure") of the material. [Electrical conductivity](/wiki/Electrical_conductivity "Electrical conductivity") arises due to the presence of electrons in states that are [delocalized](/wiki/Delocalized_electron "Delocalized electron") (extending through the material), however in order to transport electrons a state must be *partially filled*, containing an electron only part of the time.[[22]](#cite_note-22) If the state is always occupied with an electron, then it is inert, blocking the passage of other electrons via that state. The energies of these quantum states are critical since a state is partially filled only if its energy is near the [Fermi level](/wiki/Fermi_level "Fermi level")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] (see [Fermi–Dirac statistics](/wiki/Fermi%E2%80%93Dirac_statistics "Fermi–Dirac statistics")).

High conductivity in material comes from it having many partially filled states and much state delocalization.
Metals are good [electrical conductors](/wiki/Electrical_conductor "Electrical conductor") and have many partially filled states with energies near their Fermi level.
[Insulators](/wiki/Insulator_(electricity) "Insulator (electricity)"), by contrast, have few partially filled states, their Fermi levels sit within [band gaps](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap") with few energy states to occupy. Importantly, an insulator can be made to conduct by increasing its temperature: heating provides energy to promote some electrons across the band gap, inducing partially filled states in both the band of states beneath the band gap ([valence band](/wiki/Valence_band "Valence band")) and the band of states above the band gap ([conduction band](/wiki/Conduction_band "Conduction band")). An (intrinsic) semiconductor has a band gap that is smaller than that of an insulator and at room temperature, significant numbers of electrons can be excited to cross the band gap.[[23]](#cite_note-Kittel-23)

A pure semiconductor, however, is not very useful, as it is neither a very good insulator nor a very good conductor.
However, one important feature of semiconductors (and some insulators, known as *semi-insulators*) is that their conductivity can be increased and controlled by [doping](/wiki/Doping_(semiconductor) "Doping (semiconductor)") with impurities and [gating](/wiki/Field_effect_(semiconductor) "Field effect (semiconductor)") with electric fields. Doping and gating move either the conduction or valence band much closer to the Fermi level and greatly increase the number of partially filled states.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some [wider-bandgap semiconductor](/wiki/Wide-bandgap_semiconductor "Wide-bandgap semiconductor") materials are sometimes referred to as **semi-insulators**. When undoped, these have electrical conductivity nearer to that of electrical insulators, however they can be doped (making them as useful as semiconductors). Semi-insulators find niche applications in micro-electronics, such as substrates for [HEMT](/wiki/High-electron-mobility_transistor "High-electron-mobility transistor"). An example of a common semi-insulator is [gallium arsenide](/wiki/Gallium_arsenide "Gallium arsenide").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Some materials, such as [titanium dioxide](/wiki/Titanium_dioxide "Titanium dioxide"), can even be used as insulating materials for some applications, while being treated as wide-gap semiconductors for other applications.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Charge carriers (electrons and holes)

The partial filling of the states at the bottom of the conduction band can be understood as adding electrons to that band. The electrons do not stay indefinitely (due to the natural thermal [recombination](/wiki/Recombination_(physics) "Recombination (physics)")) but they can move around for some time. The actual concentration of electrons is typically very dilute, and so (unlike in metals) it is possible to think of the electrons in the conduction band of a semiconductor as a sort of classical [ideal gas](/wiki/Ideal_gas "Ideal gas"), where the electrons fly around freely without being subject to the [Pauli exclusion principle](/wiki/Pauli_exclusion_principle "Pauli exclusion principle"). In most semiconductors, the conduction bands have a parabolic [dispersion relation](/wiki/Dispersion_relation "Dispersion relation"), and so these electrons respond to forces (electric field, magnetic field, etc.) much as they would in a vacuum, though with a different [effective mass](/wiki/Effective_mass_(solid-state_physics) "Effective mass (solid-state physics)").[[23]](#cite_note-Kittel-23) Because the electrons behave like an ideal gas, one may also think about conduction in very simplistic terms such as the [Drude model](/wiki/Drude_model "Drude model"), and introduce concepts such as [electron mobility](/wiki/Electron_mobility "Electron mobility").

For partial filling at the top of the valence band, it is helpful to introduce the concept of an [electron hole](/wiki/Electron_hole "Electron hole"). Although the electrons in the valence band are always moving around, a completely full valence band is inert, not conducting any current. If an electron is taken out of the valence band, then the trajectory that the electron would normally have taken is now missing its charge. For the purposes of electric current, this combination of the full valence band, minus the electron, can be converted into a picture of a completely empty band containing a positively charged particle that moves in the same way as the electron. Combined with the *negative* effective mass of the electrons at the top of the valence band, we arrive at a picture of a positively charged particle that responds to electric and magnetic fields just as a normal positively charged particle would do in a vacuum, again with some positive effective mass.[[23]](#cite_note-Kittel-23) This particle is called a hole, and the collection of holes in the valence band can again be understood in simple classical terms (as with the electrons in the conduction band).

#### Carrier generation and recombination

When [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation") strikes a semiconductor, it may excite an electron out of its energy level and consequently leave a hole. This process is known as [*electron-hole pair generation*](/wiki/Carrier_generation_and_recombination "Carrier generation and recombination"). Electron-hole pairs are constantly generated from [thermal energy](/wiki/Thermal_energy "Thermal energy") as well, in the absence of any external energy source.

Electron-hole pairs are also apt to recombine. [Conservation of energy](/wiki/Conservation_of_energy "Conservation of energy") demands that these recombination events, in which an electron loses an amount of [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy") larger than the [band gap](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap"), be accompanied by the emission of thermal energy (in the form of [phonons](/wiki/Phonon "Phonon")) or radiation (in the form of [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon")).

In some states, the generation and recombination of electron–hole pairs are in equipoise. The number of electron-hole pairs in the [steady state](/wiki/Steady_state "Steady state") at a given temperature is determined by [quantum statistical mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_statistical_mechanics "Quantum statistical mechanics"). The precise [quantum mechanical](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") mechanisms of generation and recombination are governed by the [conservation of energy](/wiki/Conservation_of_energy "Conservation of energy") and [conservation of momentum](/wiki/Conservation_of_momentum "Conservation of momentum").

As the probability that electrons and holes meet together is proportional to the product of their numbers, the product is in the steady-state nearly constant at a given temperature, providing that there is no significant electric field (which might "flush" carriers of both types, or move them from neighbor regions containing more of them to meet together) or externally driven pair generation. The product is a function of the temperature, as the probability of getting enough thermal energy to produce a pair increases with temperature, being approximately exp(−*E*G/*kT*), where *k* is the [Boltzmann constant](/wiki/Boltzmann_constant "Boltzmann constant"), *T* is the absolute temperature and *E*G is bandgap.

The probability of meeting is increased by carrier traps – impurities or dislocations which can trap an electron or hole and hold it until a pair is completed. Such carrier traps are sometimes purposely added to reduce the time needed to reach the steady-state.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Doping

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Silicon_doping_-_Type_P_and_N.svg/500px-Silicon_doping_-_Type_P_and_N.svg.png)

The conductivity of semiconductors may easily be modified by introducing impurities into their [crystal lattice](/wiki/Crystal_lattice "Crystal lattice"). The process of adding controlled impurities to a semiconductor is known as **doping**. The amount of impurity, or dopant, added to an *[intrinsic](/wiki/Intrinsic_semiconductor "Intrinsic semiconductor")* (pure) semiconductor varies its level of conductivity.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Doped semiconductors are referred to as [*extrinsic*](/wiki/Extrinsic_semiconductor "Extrinsic semiconductor").[[27]](#cite_note-27) By adding impurity to the pure semiconductors, the electrical conductivity may be varied by factors of thousands or millions.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

A 1 cm3 specimen of a metal or semiconductor has the order of 1022 atoms.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In a metal, every atom donates at least one free electron for conduction, thus 1 cm3 of metal contains on the order of 1022 free electrons,[[30]](#cite_note-30) whereas a 1 cm3 sample of pure germanium at 20 °C contains about 4.2×1022 atoms, but only 2.5×1013 free electrons and 2.5×1013 holes. The addition of 0.001% of [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic "Arsenic") (an impurity) donates an extra 1017 free electrons in the same volume and the electrical conductivity is increased by a factor of 10,000.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The materials chosen as suitable dopants depend on the atomic properties of both the dopant and the material to be doped. In general, dopants that produce the desired controlled changes are classified as either electron [acceptors](/wiki/Acceptor_(semiconductors) "Acceptor (semiconductors)") or [donors](/wiki/Donor_(semiconductors) "Donor (semiconductors)"). Semiconductors doped with *donor* impurities are called *n-type*, while those doped with *acceptor* impurities are known as *p-type*. The n and p type designations indicate which charge carrier acts as the material's [majority carrier](/wiki/Majority_carrier "Majority carrier"). The opposite carrier is called the [minority carrier](/wiki/Minority_carrier "Minority carrier"), which exists due to thermal excitation at a much lower concentration compared to the majority carrier.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

For example, the pure semiconductor [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") has four valence electrons that bond each silicon atom to its neighbors.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In silicon, the most common dopants are [group III](/wiki/Boron_group "Boron group") and [group V](/wiki/Group_V "Group V") elements. Group III elements all contain three valence electrons, causing them to function as acceptors when used to dope silicon. When an acceptor atom replaces a silicon atom in the crystal, a vacant state (an electron "hole") is created, which can move around the lattice and function as a charge carrier. Group V elements have five valence electrons, which allows them to act as a donor; substitution of these atoms for silicon creates an extra free electron. Therefore, a silicon crystal doped with [boron](/wiki/Boron "Boron") creates a p-type semiconductor whereas one doped with [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus") results in an n-type material.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

During [manufacture](/wiki/Semiconductor_device_fabrication "Semiconductor device fabrication"), dopants can be diffused into the semiconductor body by contact with gaseous compounds of the desired element, or [ion implantation](/wiki/Ion_implantation "Ion implantation") can be used to accurately position the doped regions.

### Amorphous semiconductors

Some materials, when rapidly cooled to a glassy amorphous state, have semiconducting properties. These include B, [Si](/wiki/Amorphous_silicon "Amorphous silicon"), Ge, Se, and Te, and there are multiple theories to explain them.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Early history of semiconductors

The history of the understanding of semiconductors begins with experiments on the electrical properties of materials. The properties of the time-temperature coefficient of resistance, rectification, and light-sensitivity were observed starting in the early 19th century.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Ferdinand_Braun.jpg/250px-Ferdinand_Braun.jpg)

[Thomas Johann Seebeck](/wiki/Thomas_Johann_Seebeck "Thomas Johann Seebeck") was the first to notice that semiconductors exhibit special featur

enriched_points.expanded_content: Semiconductors are essential for modern electronics, enabling efficient control of electrical currents in devices from smartphones to computers, facilitating advancements across various industries.

enriched_points.point_title: Comparison of Silicon vs. WBG Materials
enriched_points.expanded_content: Silicon, while widely used, limits performance at high temperatures and voltages. Wide-bandgap (WBG) materials like gallium nitride and silicon carbide offer superior efficiency and thermal stability.
Semiconductor technology evolved from early 20th-century discoveries to the invention of transistors in 1947, driving rapid advancements in electronics and communication technologies.
